plainEnglish: Every image, icon, chart, or other non-text element needs a text alternative that conveys the same meaning. Informative images get descriptive alt text; purely decorative ones get an empty alt (alt="") so screen readers skip them; icon buttons and controls need an accessible name. The goal is that someone who can't see the visual still gets the equivalent information or function.
whyItMatters: Screen reader users—including the roughly 250 million people worldwide with significant vision loss—get nothing useful when an image has no alt text, or hear a meaningless filename like "IMG_4032.jpg" read aloud.
quickCheck: In Chrome DevTools, inspect each and icon button in the Elements panel and confirm it has meaningful alt text or an aria-label, or run the axe DevTools extension, which flags image-alt violations instantly.
commonMistakes: Leaving the alt attribute off an entirely, so screen readers announce the file name instead.
Using a <div onClick> or an icon <span> as a button with no aria-label, so it's announced as nothing or as 'group'.
Writing redundant alt text like alt="image of a chart" instead of describing what the chart actually shows.
Giving decorative images descriptive alt text instead of alt="", forcing screen reader users to hear clutter.
Embedding meaningful text in an SVG or icon font with no accessible name (role="img" plus aria-label).

codeExamples.label: Informative image with descriptive alt text
